text: This is Logan Mellert with National Surgery Review. Thank you for joining us today as we walk you through a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon accesses the abdomen at Palmer's point using an optical trocar. A 5mm trocar is placed at the umbilicus, and two 3mm trocars are positioned along the right costal margin. Atraumatic graspers are used to grasp and retract the gallbladder cephalad and laterally, exposing the triangle of Calot. The initial inspection reveals the cystic duct, common hepatic duct, and common bile duct.

The surgeon begins careful dissection using an L-hook electrocautery, hooking adhesions and applying anterior tension to avoid injury to the duodenum or ductal structures. Perineal attachments behind the gallbladder are meticulously dissected, followed by a pause to identify anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to carefully dissect the cystic duct within the triangle of Calot, which is defined by the common hepatic duct medially, the cystic duct laterally, and the inferior edge of the liver superiorly. The cystic artery is identified on the gallbladder wall, guiding the dissection process as attachments are separated from the front and back of the cystic duct.

The surgeon focuses on the cystic artery after fully dissecting the cystic duct. The critical view of safety is achieved by clearing the hepatocystic triangle of fat and fibrinous tissues, revealing two structures entering the gallbladder and exposing the lower third of the cystic plate. Plastic clips are applied to ligate the cystic duct, with two clips placed proximally and one distally.

The surgeon uses hook scissors to divide the cystic duct, minimizing the risk of injury. The cystic artery is doubly clipped and divided distally with electrocautery. A small posterior cystic artery branch is identified and similarly clipped and divided.

The surgeon proceeds with the dissection of the gallbladder from the liver bed using electrocautery, carefully maintaining the areolar plane to avoid liver bed bleeding or gallbladder entry. Liver bed bleeding is promptly controlled with electrocautery. In cases of acute cholecystitis, the plane is more distinct due to tissue edema.

The surgeon removes the gallbladder from the liver bed, grasping it at the cystic duct and pulling it through the trocar without a bag. The liver bed is reinspected for hemostasis before desufflation.
